claim: Durable execution guarantees a workflow runs to completion despite failures by persisting an event history and replaying on restart.
quote: Durable execution guarantees that all executions of all processes will run to completion successfully, in spite of failures.

claim: Temporal's three message types: Signal (async, fire-and-forget), Query (read-only), Update (sync write with validation) — Update fits human approval flows.
quote: Signals don't kick off any long-running operations or otherwise block. They're guaranteed to run atomically. Queries are not allowed to block. Updates provide three-phase handling: validation, acceptance into history, and completion with a return value.

claim: Three architectures: Temporal needs a separate cluster, Restate is a sidecar, DBOS is a Postgres-backed library.
quote: Temporal: Separate cluster with dedicated persistence layer. Restate: Lightweight sidecar or embedded library using HTTP/gRPC. DBOS: PostgreSQL-backed, uses existing databases as the durable execution backend.

claim: Temporal is the most-starred mature durable-execution platform.
quote: Temporal is a durable execution system that transforms unreliable code into reliable software.

claim: Restate ships as a single binary and embeds RocksDB; no separate cluster or DB needed.
quote: Restate acts as a proxy layer in front of your existing services, storing journal state in its own embedded RocksDB.

claim: DBOS argues Postgres is enough — locking clauses give high-throughput dequeues; transactions make step+checkpoint atomic.
quote: By greatly reducing contention, Postgres enables a durable workflow system to process tens of thousands of workflows per second across thousands of workers.

claim: DBOS Transact (TypeScript) is a lightweight library — your app + Postgres, no orchestration server.
quote: Lightweight Durable TypeScript Workflows

claim: DBOS Transact (Python) provides the same Postgres-backed durable workflow library.
quote: Lightweight Durable Python Workflows

claim: Inngest persists each step result so retries skip completed steps.
quote: Inngest treats each step within a function as a durable, transactional unit. If the function fails and is retried, it re-runs from the beginning, but it skips any steps that have already completed successfully.

claim: Trigger.dev v3 runs jobs on dedicated compute, no serverless timeout, Apache 2.0 self-hostable.
quote: Trigger.dev v3 runs jobs on dedicated compute with no serverless timeout limits.

claim: Hatchet bundles a durable task queue, dashboard, and CLI on top of Postgres; aimed at >100/s throughput.
quote: Hatchet is a platform for running background tasks and durable workflows, built on top of Postgres.

claim: Trigger.dev positions as easier-to-use Temporal; Inngest is event-driven serverless-first; Temporal is the battle-tested standard.
quote: Trigger.dev: described as easier-to-use Temporal with integrations. Inngest: building durable workflows on a purely event-driven model designed as serverless-first.

claim: Cloudflare Workflows persists state between steps so each step is independently retriable.
quote: Workflows implements a step-based architecture where each step in your application is independently retriable, with state automatically persisted between steps.

claim: Cloudflare Workflows reached GA as production-ready durable execution on Workers.
quote: Cloudflare Workflows is now GA: production-ready durable execution.

claim: Workflows steps include step.do, step.sleep, step.sleepUntil, and step.waitForEvent for human-in-the-loop pauses.
quote: Each step can either execute code (step.do), put your Workflow to sleep (step.sleep or step.sleepUntil), or wait on an event (step.waitForEvent).

claim: Durable Objects pair compute with strongly-consistent SQLite storage routed to a unique global instance — fits per-thread state.
quote: Each Durable Object has some durable storage attached. Since this storage lives together with the object, it is strongly consistent yet fast to access.

claim: GitHub-hosted runner jobs cap at 6 h; self-hosted at 5 days; a workflow run cannot exceed 35 days including waits.
quote: Each job in a workflow can run for up to 6 hours of execution time. If a job reaches this limit, the job is terminated and fails.

claim: Reusable workflows chain up to 10 levels; outputs are typed; build artifacts are the only way to share files; secrets do not auto-propagate.
quote: You can connect a maximum of ten levels of workflows. Secrets are only passed to directly called workflow.

claim: GitHub Actions outputs cap at 1 MB per job and 50 MB total per run, encoded as UTF-16.
quote: Outputs can be a maximum of 1 MB per job, and the total of all outputs in a workflow run can be a maximum of 50 MB.

claim: Temporal workflows hit hard limits at 50,000 events or 50 MB of history; very-long flows need Continue-As-New.
quote: There are two limits in Temporal that keep a single Workflow execution from actually running forever: 50Ki Events in the Event History and an Event History size of 50MB.

claim: Slack bots use Redis for per-thread leases to deduplicate events and prevent two workers replying to the same thread.
quote: Apps use Redis to deduplicate events and acquire per-thread leases, which prevents duplicate replies and race conditions across workers.

claim: Slack Bolt does not ship durable state; persistence is the developer's problem.
quote: A framework to build Slack apps using JavaScript

claim: Dagu is a single-binary self-hosted workflow engine with no required external DB or message broker.
quote: Dagu is a single binary with no required database, message broker, or control-plane stack.

claim: Inngest can run as a single binary with embedded SQLite — useful for self-hosted homelab orchestration.
quote: The inngest start command runs all system services in one process with no external database or queue dependencies, with SQLite handling persistence by writing system state to disk.

claim: Windmill is an open-source Postgres-backed workflow engine — every state transition is one ACID Postgres statement.
quote: Windmill's state machine design leverages the property that every state transition is just one transacted PostgreSQL statement.

claim: DB-backed finite state machines remain the simplest way to model multi-step approval flows when full durable-execution is overkill.
quote: Use your database to power state machines.

claim: Anthropic's claude-code-action is the canonical way to drive Claude Code from a GitHub Actions runner.
quote: Claude Code GitHub Actions brings AI-powered automation to your GitHub workflow.

claim: Durable execution engines complement event logs (Kafka) — log handles transport, engine handles workflow lifecycle.
quote: A durable execution engine ensures reliable, stateful execution of workflows and processes in distributed systems.
